name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْعَدْلِ وَٱلْإِحْسَـٰنِ وَإِيتَآئِ ذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَيَنْهَىٰ عَنِ ٱلْفَحْشَآءِ وَٱلْمُنكَرِ وَٱلْبَغْىِ ۚ يَعِظُكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (٩٠)
```

```english
Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness, and generosity towards relatives, and He forbids indecency, wrongdoing, and aggression. He advises you, so that you may take admonition. (90)
```

```arabic
وَأَوْفُوا۟ بِعَهْدِ ٱللَّهِ إِذَا عَـٰهَدتُّمْ وَلَا تَنقُضُوا۟ ٱلْأَيْمَـٰنَ بَعْدَ تَوْكِيدِهَا وَقَدْ جَعَلْتُمُ ٱللَّهَ عَلَيْكُمْ كَفِيلًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا تَفْعَلُونَ (٩١)
```

```english
Fulfill Allah’s covenant when you pledge, and do not break [your] oaths, after pledging them solemnly and having made Allah a witness over yourselves. Indeed Allah knows what you do. (91)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّتِى نَقَضَتْ غَزْلَهَا مِنۢ بَعْدِ قُوَّةٍ أَنكَـٰثًا تَتَّخِذُونَ أَيْمَـٰنَكُمْ دَخَلًۢا بَيْنَكُمْ أَن تَكُونَ أُمَّةٌ هِىَ أَرْبَىٰ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ ۚ إِنَّمَا يَبْلُوكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦ ۚ وَلَيُبَيِّنَنَّ لَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ (٩٢)
```

```english
Do not be like her who would undo her yarn, breaking it up after [spinning it to] strength, by making your oaths a means of [mutual] deceit among yourselves, so that one community may become more affluent than another community. Indeed Allah tests you thereby, and He will surely clarify for you on the Day of Resurrection what you used to differ about. (92)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً وَلَـٰكِن يُضِلُّ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَلَتُسْـَٔلُنَّ عَمَّا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٣)
```

```english
Had Allah wished, He would have made you one community, but He leads astray whomever He wishes and guides whomever He wishes, and you will surely be questioned concerning what you used to do. (93)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَتَّخِذُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَكُمْ دَخَلًۢا بَيْنَكُمْ فَتَزِلَّ قَدَمٌۢ بَعْدَ ثُبُوتِهَا وَتَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلسُّوٓءَ بِمَا صَدَدتُّمْ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَلَكُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (٩٤)
```

```english
Do not make your oaths a means of [mutual] deceit among yourselves, lest feet should stumble after being steady and [lest] you suffer ill for barring from the way of Allah and face a great punishment. (94)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَشْتَرُوا۟ بِعَهْدِ ٱللَّهِ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۚ إِنَّمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ هُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٩٥)
```

```english
Do not sell Allah’s covenants for a paltry gain. Indeed, what is with Allah is better for you, should you know. (95)
```

```arabic
مَا عِندَكُمْ يَنفَدُ ۖ وَمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ بَاقٍ ۗ وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوٓا۟ أَجْرَهُم بِأَحْسَنِ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٩٦)
```

```english
That which is with you will be spent [and gone], but what is with Allah shall last, and We will surely pay the patient their reward by the best of what they used to do. (96)
```

```arabic
مَنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا مِّن ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنثَىٰ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُۥ حَيَوٰةً طَيِّبَةً ۖ وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْرَهُم بِأَحْسَنِ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٩٧)
```

```english
Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall revive him with a good life and pay them their reward by the best of what they used to do. (97)
```

```arabic
فَإِذَا قَرَأْتَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ٱلرَّجِيمِ (٩٨)
```

```english
When you recite the Quran, seek the protection of Allah against the outcast Satan. (98)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُۥ لَيْسَ لَهُۥ سُلْطَـٰنٌ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ (٩٩)
```

```english
Indeed he does not have any authority over those who have faith and put their trust in their Lord. (99)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَا سُلْطَـٰنُهُۥ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُۥ وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِهِۦ مُشْرِكُونَ (١٠٠)
```

```english
His authority is only over those who befriend him and those who make him a partner [of Allah]. (100)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا بَدَّلْنَآ ءَايَةً مَّكَانَ ءَايَةٍ ۙ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يُنَزِّلُ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مُفْتَرٍۭ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٠١)
```

```english
When We change a sign for another in its stead—and Allah knows best what He sends down—they say, ‘You are indeed a fabricator.’ Indeed, most of them do not know. (101)
```

```arabic
قُلْ نَزَّلَهُۥ رُوحُ ٱلْقُدُسِ مِن رَّبِّكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ لِيُثَبِّتَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَهُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُسْلِمِينَ (١٠٢)
```

```english
Say, the Holy Spirit has brought it down duly from your Lord to fortify those who have faith and as guidance and good news for the Muslims. (102)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُۥ بَشَرٌ ۗ لِّسَانُ ٱلَّذِى يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجَمِىٌّ وَهَـٰذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِىٌّ مُّبِينٌ (١٠٣)
```

```english
We certainly know that they say, ‘It is only a human that instructs him.’ The language of him to whom they refer is non-Arabic, while this is a clear Arabic language. (103)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ لَا يَهْدِيهِمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٠٤)
```

```english
Indeed those who do not believe in the signs of Allah—Allah shall not guide them and there is a painful punishment for them. (104)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَا يَفْتَرِى ٱلْكَذِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰذِبُونَ (١٠٥)
```

```english
Only those fabricate lies who do not believe in the signs of Allah, and it is they who are the liars. (105)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses mention several rulings that align with the state of Islam before the Migration (الهجرة), which serve to rectify the condition of the general society. These include the command for justice and excellence, and the prohibition of indecency, wrongdoing, and transgression. Related to this are the command to give to kinsfolk and the prohibition against breaking covenants and oaths. The verses also mention other matters that suit and reinforce these principles.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسانِ وَإِيتاءِ ذِي الْقُرْبى** — "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and dedicated ethical conduct, and giving to kinsfolk" (16:90). The Glorified began with these three rulings because they are, in order, the most important pillars upon which the backbone of human society stands. This is because the rectification of the general society is the most important objective sought by Islam in its reformative teachings. Although the most important thing to a human being from the perspective of nature (الطبيعة) is his own individual self, the happiness of the person is built upon the rectification of the social environment (الظرف الاجتماعي) in which he lives. How difficult it is for an individual to succeed in a corrupt society where wretchedness surrounds him from every side.

For this reason, Islam has paid such attention to the rectification of society that no other system equals it. It has exerted immense effort in making religious statutes and teachings—even acts of worship (العبادات) like prayer (الصلاة), pilgrimage (الحج), and fasting (الصوم)—social in nature as much as possible. All of this is to rectify the human being within himself and from the perspective of his life's environment.

Thus, His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ** — "Indeed Allah enjoins justice" is a command for justice (العدل), the opposite of which is injustice (الظلم). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "Justice (العدالة) and balancing (المعادلة) are terms that imply the meaning of equality (المساواة)... *al-Adl* (العدل) with a *fatha* on the *ayn* and *al-Idl* (العدل) with a *kasra* are close in meaning. However, *al-Adl* (with *fatha*) is used for that which is perceived by insight (البصيرة), such as rulings; hence His saying, the Exalted: **أَوْ عَدْلُ ذلِكَ صِياماً** — 'or the equivalent (عدل) of that in fasting' (5:95). *Al-Idl* (with *kasra*) and *al-Adil* (العديل) are used for that which is perceived by the senses, such as things weighed, counted, or measured. Thus, justice (العدل) is distributing equally."

He continued: "Justice is of two types: absolute justice, the goodness of which is necessitated by the intellect (العقل), which is never abrogated in any era and cannot be described as transgression in any way—such as doing good to one who does good to you and refraining from harming one who refrains from harming you. The second is justice known to be such through the Law (الشرع), which can be abrogated in some eras, such as retribution (القصاص), indemnities for injuries (أروش الجنايات), and the original property of an apostate. Regarding this, He said: **فَمَنِ اعْتَدى عَلَيْكُمْ فَاعْتَدُوا عَلَيْهِ** — 'So should anyone aggress against you, assail him' (2:194), and He said: **وَجَزاءُ سَيِّئَةٍ سَيِّئَةٌ مِثْلُها** — 'The recompense of evil is evil like it' (42:40); thus it was called 'aggression' and 'evil'."

This latter type is what is intended by His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسانِ** — "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and dedicated ethical conduct." For justice (العدل) is equality in recompense—good for good and evil for evil—while excellence (الإحسان) is to meet good with more than its like and evil with less than its like. Here ends the relevant portion [of Al-Raghib's words].

What he mentioned, despite its detail, returns to the common saying that justice (العدل) is adhering to the middle path (الوسط) and avoiding the two extremes of excess (الإفراط) and deficiency (التفريط) in all matters. This is a type of explanation by the necessary implication of the meaning (لازم المعنى). The reality of justice (حقيقة العدل) is the establishment of equality (المساواة) and balance (الموازنة) between matters, such that each share is given what it ought to be given, resulting in equality in that each thing is placed in its rightful position. Thus, justice in belief (الاعتقاد) is to believe in what is the truth (الحق). Justice in a person's action regarding himself is to do what leads to his happiness and to avoid what leads to his wretchedness by following the soul's whims (هوى النفس). Justice among people is to place everyone in the position they deserve according to the intellect (العقل), the Law (الشرع), or custom (العرف); thus the doer of good is rewarded for his excellence, the evildoer is punished for his misdeed, the oppressed is given justice against the oppressor, and there is no partiality or exception in the implementation of the law.

From this, it becomes clear that justice (العدل) is synonymous with and inseparable from beauty/goodness (الحسن), for we mean nothing by beauty (الحسن) except that which the soul is naturally inclined toward and attracted to. Placing a thing in its proper position is something a human being naturally inclines toward, recognizes as beautiful, and offers excuses for if he violates it to one who rebukes him. No two people differ on this, even if people differ greatly in its practical applications (مصاديقه) according to their different paths in life.

It also appears that what Al-Raghib counted in his words as "aggression" and "evil" being "justice" is not without some figurative looseness (مسامحة). For the aggression and evil with which the aggressor and evildoer are recompensed are only aggression and evil in relation to them; as for the one who recompenses them, these acts are part of adhering to the middle path of moderation (الاعتدال) and the trait of beauty (الحسن), because they constitute placing a thing in its proper position.

In any case, although justice (العدل) is divided into a person's justice within himself and his justice toward others—which are individual justice (العدل الفردي) and social justice (العدل الاجتماعي)—and the term is absolute, the apparent context (السياق) suggests that what is intended in the verse is social justice. This means treating every member of society according to what they deserve and placing them in their proper position. This is a command for a social trait directed at individual legally responsible persons (المكلفين), meaning that Allah, the Glorified, commands every individual member of society to practice justice. By necessity, the command also pertains to the collective, such that society is tasked with establishing this ruling, and the government (الحكومة) undertakes it insofar as it manages and administers the affairs of society.

His saying: **وَالْإِحْسانِ** — "and dedicated ethical conduct (الإحسان)": The discussion regarding it, in terms of the context's requirement, is like the previous one. What is intended is excellence toward others, rather than excellence in the sense of performing an act beautifully. It is the conveying of good or benefit to another not by way of recompense or reciprocity—such as meeting good with more than its like and evil with less than its like, as mentioned before—and conveying good to another voluntarily and initiatively.

Excellence (الإحسان), despite its role in rectifying the state of those humiliated by misery and poverty or forced by calamities, and its role in spreading mercy and creating love, sees its praiseworthy effects return to the soul of the benefactor through the circulation of wealth in society and the attainment of security and peace through the fostering of affection.

His saying: **وَإِيتاءِ ذِي الْقُرْبى** — "and giving to kinsfolk": That is, giving wealth to relatives. This is one of the specific instances of excellence (الإحسان) that has been singled out for mention to indicate extra care for the rectification of this small society [the family], which is in reality the cause for the formation of the large civil society. Just as the marital society, which is smaller than the kinship society, is a prior formative cause for it, great civil societies only began from a domestic society formed by marriage, then expanded through procreation and lineage until it became a tribe and a clan, and continued to increase and multiply until it became a great nation. Thus, "kinsfolk" (ذي القربى) refers to the genus rather than a specific individual, and it is general for all kinship as scholars have mentioned.

In the narrated exegesis from the Imams of the People of the House (عليه‌السلام), it is mentioned that "kinsfolk" (ذي القربى) refers to the Imam from the progeny of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and "giving" (الإيتاء) refers to giving the *khums* (الخمس) — *the one-fifth tax* — which Allah, the Glorified, made obligatory in His saying: **وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما غَنِمْتُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فَأَنَّ لِلَّهِ خُمُسَهُ وَلِلرَّسُولِ وَلِذِي الْقُرْبى وَالْيَتامى وَالْمَساكِينِ** — "Know that whatever thing you may come by, a fifth of it is for Allah and the Apostle, for the relatives and the orphans, for the needy" (8:41). The exegesis of this has already preceded.

Perhaps the use of the singular form where it says **ذِي الْقُرْبى** (the relative) rather than the plural **ذوي القربى** or **أولي القربى** (the relatives)—as in His saying: **وَإِذا حَضَرَ الْقِسْمَةَ أُولُوا الْقُرْبى وَالْيَتامى وَالْمَساكِينُ** — "And when the relatives, the orphans and the needy are present at the division" (4:8), and His saying: **وَآتَى الْمالَ عَلى حُبِّهِ ذَوِي الْقُرْبى وَالْيَتامى وَالْمَساكِينَ** — "and gives his wealth, for the love of Him, to relatives, orphans, the needy" (2:177)—supports this [interpretation].

The possibility that "kinsfolk" (ذي القربى) refers to the genus is weakened by what occurs in the context of the verse of *khums*, where orphans and the needy are mentioned alongside it in the plural form, without any apparent subtle point (نكتة) specific to the relatives, orphans, or needy that would necessitate such a distinction.

Furthermore, there is no clear indicator (قرينة) in the verse that "giving" (الإيتاء) means excellence (الإحسان), and then by excellence, absolute excellence. And Allah knows best.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَيَنْهى عَنِ الْفَحْشاءِ وَالْمُنْكَرِ وَالْبَغْيِ يَعِظُكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ** — "and He forbids indecency, wrongdoing, and transgression. He advises you so that you may take admonition" (16:90). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "Indecency (الفحش), lewdness (الفحشاء), and the foul deed (الفاحشة) refer to that which is excessively ugly among actions and words." Perhaps the root of its meaning is exceeding the limit in what is improper; it is said: "an excessive loss" (غبن فاحش), meaning one that exceeds the limit of endurance, patience, and silence.

Wrongdoing (المنكر) — *that which is socially or religiously rejected* — refers to actions that people do not recognize in their society, which are abandoned by them due to their ugliness or sinfulness, such as public sexual acts or exposing the private parts (العورة) in the presence of people in Islamic societies.

Transgression (البغي) — *the act of seeking or overstepping* — has its root meaning in "seeking" (الطلب), and it is frequently used for seeking the right of another by encroaching upon it. Thus, it conveys the meaning of seeking superiority (الاستعلاء) and arrogance (الاستكبار) over others through injustice and insolence. Sometimes it means adultery, but what is intended in the verse is encroaching upon others through injustice.

These three—indecency (الفحشاء), wrongdoing (المنكر), and transgression (البغي)—though they often overlap in their practical instances (المصاديق), for every indecency is a wrongdoing and most transgression is indecency and wrongdoing, the prohibition is attached to them by virtue of these specific titles. This is because the occurrence of actions under these titles in any society causes a foul separation (الفصل الفاحش) between the collective actions of its people. Consequently, they become disconnected from one another, their cohesion is nullified, the order is corrupted, and the society effectively dissolves, even if it appears to stand. In this lies the destruction of individual happiness.

Thus, the prohibition of indecency, wrongdoing, and transgression is, in meaning, a command for the unity of a society whose parts recognize one another and whose actions harmonize—where some do not seek superiority over others through transgression, and where they see nothing from one another except the beautiful (الجميل) that they recognize, neither indecency nor wrongdoing. At that point, mercy, love, and intimacy settle upon them, strength and intensity are rooted within them, and resentment, enmity, aversion, and every evil trait leading to division and destruction depart from them.

Then He, the Glorified, concluded the verse with His saying: **يَعِظُكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ** — "He advises you so that you may take admonition" (16:90), meaning so that you may remember and know that what He calls you toward contains your life and your happiness.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَأَوْفُوا بِعَهْدِ اللهِ إِذا عاهَدْتُمْ وَلا تَنْقُضُوا الْأَيْمانَ بَعْدَ تَوْكِيدِها** — "Fulfill Allah’s covenant when you pledge, and do not break [your] oaths after pledging them" (16:91). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "The covenant (العهد) is the preservation of a thing and its observance state after state. The pact that must be observed is called a covenant." He said: "So-and-so covenanted (عهد) with so-and-so, meaning he delivered the covenant to him and enjoined him to preserve it."

The apparent meaning of attributing the covenant to Allah in His saying: **وَأَوْفُوا بِعَهْدِ اللهِ** — "Fulfill Allah’s covenant" is that it refers to the covenant in which Allah is made a party, rather than absolute covenants. A similar discussion applies to the breaking of oaths.

His saying: **وَلا تَنْقُضُوا الْأَيْمانَ بَعْدَ تَوْكِيدِها** — "and do not break [your] oaths after pledging them": Breaking an oath (نقض اليمين) is to violate it and act contrary to its requirement. What is intended by "oath" (اليمين) is the oath sworn by Allah, the Glorified, as if anything else is not considered an oath. The evidence for this is His subsequent saying: **وَقَدْ جَعَلْتُمُ اللهَ عَلَيْكُمْ كَفِيلاً** — "now that you have made Allah your surety" (16:91). What is intended by "pledging them" (توكيدها) is making them firm through intention and resolve, and that they be for a preferred matter, unlike their saying "No, by Allah" and "Yes, by Allah" and other idle oaths (لغو الأيمان). Thus, "pledging" (التوكيد) in this verse conveys what "binding" (التعقيد) conveys in His saying, the Exalted: **لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ اللهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ وَلكِنْ يُؤاخِذُكُمْ بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ** — "Allah will not take you to task for what is inane in your oaths, but He will take you to task for the oaths you have bound" (5:89).

Breaking an oath is conceptually more heinous than breaking a covenant, even though both are prohibited. Furthermore, the care given to swearing in the Islamic Law is greater, as seen in the chapter on judiciary (القضاء).

To clarify the heinousness of breaking it: the reality of the meaning of an oath (اليمين) is the creation of a special link between a verbal proposition—whether informative or performative—and a significant, noble matter, such that the nullification of the proposition (through the appearance of its falsehood if it is informative, or the violation of its requirement if it is a resolve, command, or prohibition) would necessitate the loss of the dignity and honor that the swearer believes the one sworn by possesses. For example, saying: "By Allah, I shall surely do such-and-such," or "By Allah, do or do not do such-and-such." The matter results in the one sworn by, with His dignity and honor, being the one responsible for the truth of the verbal proposition, and the swearer being responsible before the one sworn by, since he linked the truth of the proposition to His dignity and honor. This is like one who enters a contract or pledges an action, then gives the other party a trusted security (موثق) of wealth, children, or otherwise, or a noble person guarantees it for him with his nobility.

Through this, the meaning of His saying, the Exalted, becomes clear: **وَقَدْ جَعَلْتُمُ اللهَ عَلَيْكُمْ كَفِيلاً** — "now that you have made Allah your surety." For the swearer, when he says, "By Allah, I shall surely do such-and-such" or "I shall surely leave such-and-such," has linked what he swore upon to Allah, the Glorified, in a certain way, making Him a surety (كفيلاً) for him in fulfilling what he bound the oath upon. If he violates it and does not fulfill it, it is for his surety to bring him to recompense and punishment. Thus, in the violation of an oath, there is an insult and a disparagement toward the presence of Might and Honor, in addition to what exists in the breaking of both oaths and covenants of severance and separation from Him, the Glorified, after the strengthening of the connection.

So His saying: **وَقَدْ جَعَلْتُمُ اللهَ** — "now that you have made Allah..." is a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to the plural pronoun in His saying: **وَلا تَنْقُضُوا** — "and do not break." His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَعْلَمُ ما تَفْعَلُونَ** — "Indeed Allah knows what you do" (16:91) is in the sense of reinforcing the prohibition by stating that the action is detestable and He is fully aware of it.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلا تَكُونُوا كَالَّتِي نَقَضَتْ غَزْلَها مِنْ بَعْدِ قُوَّةٍ أَنْكاثاً** — "Do not be like her who would undo her spinning into untwisted strands after it was strong" (16:92) to the end of the verse. Breaking (النقض), the opposite of which is firming (الإبرام), is the undoing of what was made firm of rope or yarn by twisting; thus, breaking a firmed thing is like undoing a knotted thing. *Al-Nakth* (النكث) is breaking. It is said in *al-Majma'*: "Everything that is broken after twisting is *ankath* (أنكاثاً), whether it be a rope or yarn." *Al-Dakhal* (الدخل) with two *fathas* originally refers to everything that enters a thing but is not of it; it is used as a metaphor for corruption, deception, and betrayal. *Arba* (أربى) is the elative form of *riba* (الربا), which is increase.

His saying: **وَلا تَكُونُوا كَالَّتِي نَقَضَتْ غَزْلَها مِنْ بَعْدِ قُوَّةٍ أَنْكاثاً** — "Do not be like her who would undo her spinning into untwisted strands after it was strong" is in the sense of an explanation for His saying in the previous verse: **وَلا تَنْقُضُوا الْأَيْمانَ بَعْدَ تَوْكِيدِها** — "and do not break [your] oaths after pledging them." It is a parable of a woman who spins yarn with strength, then returns and undoes what she exhausted herself in and spun after its strength, making it into untwisted strands (أنكاثاً) with no twist or firmness.

It is narrated from al-Kalbi that she was a foolish woman from Quraysh who used to spin with her maidservants until midday, then command them to undo what they had spun, and that remained her habit. Her name was Raytah bint Amr ibn Ka'b ibn Sa'd ibn Tamim ibn Murrah, and she was called the "Fool of Makkah" (خرقاء مكة).

His saying: **تَتَّخِذُونَ أَيْمانَكُمْ دَخَلاً بَيْنَكُمْ أَنْ تَكُونَ أُمَّةٌ هِيَ أَرْبى مِنْ أُمَّةٍ** — "making your oaths a means of deceit among yourselves, so that one community may become more numerous than another community" (16:92). That is, you take your oaths as a means for betrayal, deception, and treachery, putting people's minds at ease with them, then you betray and deceive them by breaking them. They only do this so that one community—the swearers—may be more numerous (أربى) and have a greater share of the vanities of the world than another community—those sworn to.

Thus, what is intended by "deceit" (الدخل) is its means, by naming the cause after the effect. **أَنْ تَكُونَ أُمَّةٌ** — "so that one community may become..." is an object of purpose (مفعول له) with the preposition *lam* implied. The speech is a type of clarification for the breaking of the oath or for their being like her who undoes her spinning into untwisted strands after it was strong. The sum of the meaning is that you are like her when you take your oaths as a means of deceit among yourselves, strengthening and binding them, then you betray and deceive by breaking and violating them, and Allah forbids you from this.

Some have mentioned that His saying: **تَتَّخِذُونَ أَيْمانَكُمْ** — "making your oaths..." is an interrogative sentence with the particle omitted, and the interrogation is for denunciation (الإنكار).

His saying: **إِنَّما يَبْلُوكُمُ اللهُ بِهِ** — "Allah only tests you by it" (16:92). That is, this is a divine trial (امتحان) by which He tests you. He has sworn that He will surely clarify for you on the Day of Resurrection that which you used to differ about. At that point, you will know the reality of your current state of scrambling for the world and following the path of falsehood to set aside the truth and refute it. On that day, it will be clear who is the strayed and who is the guided.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَوْ شاءَ اللهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً وَلكِنْ يُضِلُّ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَيهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ** — "Had Allah wished, He would have made you a single community, but He leads astray whomever He wishes and guides whomever He wishes" (16:93). When the discourse led to the mention of their differences, He followed it with a clarification that their difference does not nullify the divine purpose in their creation, nor are they beyond His power. Had He wished, He would have made them a single community with no difference between them, but Allah, the Glorified, made them different through guidance and misguidance, guiding some and misguiding others.

This is because He, the Exalted, placed human happiness and wretchedness on the basis of choice (الاختيار). He made known to them obedience (الطاعة), which leads to the ultimate happiness, and disobedience (المعصية), which leads to the ultimate wretchedness. Whoever follows the path of disobedience and passes into misguidance, Allah recompenses him with that; and whoever takes the path of obedience and chooses guidance, Allah recompenses him with that. He will surely question them all about what they did and chose.

From what has preceded, it appears that what is intended by making them a single community is the removal of difference from among them and compelling them toward guidance and happiness. By misguidance (الإضلال) and guidance (الهداية), what is meant is that which is by way of recompense, not the initial misguidance and guidance, for everyone is upon a primordial guidance (هدى فطري). Thus, the one whom Allah wishes to misguide and so misguides is the one who chose disobedience over obedience without returning or regret. The one whom Allah wished to guide and so guided is the one who remained upon his primordial guidance and proceeded upon obedience or repented and returned from disobedience—a straight path and a divine way (سنة إلهية), and you will never find any change in the way of Allah, nor will you find any alteration in the way of Allah.

Furthermore, His saying: **وَلَتُسْئَلُنَّ عَمَّا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — "and you will surely be questioned about what you used to do" (16:93) is to dispel what might occur to the imagination—that the attribution of misguidance and guidance to Him, the Glorified, nullifies the effect of their choice in that, thereby nullifying the message and making the call futile. The answer is that the questioning remains as it is, because your choice is not nullified by this; rather, Allah, the Glorified, extends for you the misguidance or guidance that you yourselves choose by leaning toward His disobedience or turning toward His obedience.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلا تَتَّخِذُوا أَيْمانَكُمْ دَخَلاً بَيْنَكُمْ فَتَزِلَّ قَدَمٌ بَعْدَ ثُبُوتِها** — "Do not make your oaths a means of deceit among yourselves, lest [your] foot should slip after being steady" (16:94) to the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "Turning away (الصدود) and averting (الصد) may be turning away from a thing and refraining, as in: **يَصُدُّونَ عَنْكَ صُدُوداً** — 'they turn away from you with aversion' (4:61). It may also be turning [others] away and preventing, as in: **وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ فَصَدَّهُمْ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ** — 'and Satan had made their deeds seem glamorous to them, and so he barred them from the way' (27:24)."

The verse is a prohibition against taking oaths as a means of deceit after the prohibition against the root of breaking oaths, because the specific act of taking them as deceit has an independent corruption (مفسدة) that is the basis for the prohibition, distinct from the corruption of the root of breaking oaths. He pointed to the corruption of the root of breaking by His saying: **وَقَدْ جَعَلْتُمُ اللهَ عَلَيْكُمْ كَفِيلاً** — "now that you have made Allah your surety" (16:91), and He points in this verse to the corruption of taking them as deceit by His saying: **فَتَزِلَّ قَدَمٌ بَعْدَ ثُبُوتِها وَتَذُوقُوا السُّوءَ بِما صَدَدْتُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَلَكُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ** — "lest [your] foot should slip after being steady, and you taste evil because you barred [others] from the way of Allah, and there be a great punishment for you" (16:94).

The two bases—as is apparent—are distinct, though one is like a prelude to the other. Just as breaking oaths is like a prelude to taking them as deceit; for when a person breaks an oath for some reason for the first time, the matter of breaking becomes easy for him, and that paves the way for breaking a second and third time, making swearing and then breaking a means of deception and treachery. He soon becomes an ally of corruption, deception, treachery, delusion, trickery, plotting, lying, and falsehood, not caring what he says or does, and becomes a germ of corruption (جرثومة فساد) that corrupts human society wherever he turns, falling into a path other than the path of Allah which the sound primordial nature (الفطرة السليمة) has charted.

In any case, the apparent meaning of His saying: **وَلا تَتَّخِذُوا أَيْمانَكُمْ دَخَلاً بَيْنَكُمْ** — "Do not make your oaths a means of deceit among yourselves" is an independent prohibition against deception through oaths after the implicit prohibition of it in the previous verse. His saying: **فَتَزِلَّ قَدَمٌ بَعْدَ ثُبُوتِها** — "lest [your] foot should slip after being steady" is a consequence (تفريع) of the prohibited act, not the prohibition itself; i.e., it follows from taking them as deceit that a foot slips after its steadiness, etc. The slipping of a foot after its steadiness is a metaphor for breaking an oath after binding and pledging it, and departing from the position in which it was rooted. For a person's steadiness and steadfastness upon what he has resolved and cared for is among the noble human traits and the foundations of its virtues, upon which the divine religion is built. Preserving an oath upon its pledging is one of the feet by which this vast foundation is completed. It is as if for this reason "foot" (قدم) was mentioned as an indefinite noun in His saying: **فَتَزِلَّ قَدَمٌ** — "lest a foot should slip."

His saying: **وَتَذُوقُوا السُّوءَ بِما صَدَدْتُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَلَكُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ** — "and you taste evil because you barred [others] from the way of Allah, and there be a great punishment for you" is conjoined to His saying: **فَتَزِلَّ قَدَمٌ** — "lest [your] foot should slip," and is a clarification of its result, just as it is a clarification of the result and consequence of His saying: **لا تَتَّخِذُوا أَيْمانَكُمْ دَخَلاً** — "Do not make your oaths a means of deceit." Through this, it appears that His saying: **بِما صَدَدْتُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ** — "because you barred [others] from the way of Allah" is in the position of an explanation for His saying: **فَتَزِلَّ قَدَمٌ بَعْدَ ثُبُوتِها** — "lest [your] foot should slip after being steady."

What is intended by "barring from the way of Allah" (الصدود عن سبيل الله) is turning away and refraining from the primordial way (السنة الفطرية) upon which Allah originated people and to which the prophetic call invited—namely, the commitment to truthfulness, steadfastness, observing covenants, pacts, and oaths, and avoiding corruption, deception, treachery, lying, falsehood, and delusion.

What is intended by "tasting evil" (ذوق السوء) is punishment. His saying: **وَلَكُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ** — "and there be a great punishment for you" is a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to the subject of "you taste" (تذوقوا). It is possible that "tasting evil" refers to the evil effects of misguidance that reach them in this world, while His saying: **وَلَكُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ** is an announcement of what will befall them in the Hereafter. This is what is gathered from the apparent meaning of the noble verse.

The meaning is: Do not take your oaths as a means of deceit among yourselves, such that it leads you to depart from what you were steady upon and to break what you made firm. In that is a turning away from the way of Allah, which is the commitment to the primordial nature and the avoidance of betrayal, deception, treachery, and corruption—in short, spreading corruption in the land after its rectification. This leads you to taste evil and wretchedness in your worldly life, and for you is a great punishment in the Hereafter.

Some have mentioned that the verse is specific to the prohibition against breaking the pledge of allegiance (بيعة) to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) according to the established practice in the early days of Islam, and that the verse was revealed concerning those who pledged allegiance to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to support Islam and its people, so Allah forbade them from breaking that pledge. According to this, "barring from the way of Allah" means turning people away and preventing them from following the religion of Allah, just as "the slipping of a foot after its steadiness" means apostasy after Islam and misguidance after right-guidance.

However, the context (السياق) does not support this, and even if it were granted, the specificity of the occasion does not negate the generality of the verse.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلا تَشْتَرُوا بِعَهْدِ اللهِ ثَمَناً قَلِيلاً إِنَّما عِنْدَ اللهِ هُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** — "Do not sell Allah’s covenant for a paltry gain. Indeed, what is with Allah is better for you, should you know" (16:95). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "Everything that is obtained as a substitute for a thing is its price (ثمنه)."

It appears that the verse is a prohibition against breaking the covenant after the command to fulfill it was given, out of concern for its importance, just as occurred with the breaking of oaths. The verse is absolute. What is intended by "Allah's covenant" (عهد الله) is the covenant in which Allah is made a party, absolutely. What is intended by "selling it for a paltry gain" (الاشتراء به ثمنا قليلا), by the evidence of the end of the verse, is to exchange the covenant for some of the vanities of the world, breaking it to attain them. The thing exchanged for was called a "price" (ثمنا) because it is a substitute, as mentioned before. The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **ما عِنْدَكُمْ يَنْفَدُ وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ باقٍ** — "That which is with you will be exhausted [and pass away], and that which is with Allah is lasting" (16:96). This is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for His saying in the previous verse: **إِنَّما عِنْدَ اللهِ هُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ** — "Indeed, what is with Allah is better for you." He justified it by stating that what is with you—meaning in the worldly life, which is a material life built on the basis of exchange and transformation, characterized by movement and change—is fleeting and exhausted. What is with Allah, the Glorified, of what He promises the God-conscious (المتقين) among you, is lasting, never to cease or perish. That which is lasting is better than that which is exhausted, by the explicit judgment of the intellect.

Know that His saying: **ما عِنْدَكُمْ يَنْفَدُ وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ باقٍ** — "That which is with you will be exhausted, and that which is with Allah is lasting," given the absolute nature of its wording, is a universal rule (قاعدة كلية) that is never broken by exception. Under it fall many particulars of true knowledge.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّ الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا أَجْرَهُمْ بِأَحْسَنِ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "and We will surely pay the patient their reward by the best of what they used to do" (16:96). Since fulfilling the covenant necessitates patience (الصبر) against the bitterness of opposing the soul's whim in breaking it and indulging in what it desires, the discourse shifted from mentioning the reward of those specifically fulfilling the covenant to mentioning the reward of those who are absolutely patient for the sake of Allah.

So His saying: **وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّ الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا أَجْرَهُمْ** — "and We will surely pay the patient their reward" is an emphasized promise for absolute patience, whether it be patience in obedience, patience from disobedience, or patience during calamity. However, it must be patience for the sake of Allah and for the sake of Allah's countenance, for the context does not support anything else.

His saying: **بِأَحْسَنِ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "by the best of what they used to do": The preposition *ba* is for reciprocity (المقابلة), as in our saying: "I sold this for (بـ) this." What is intended by "the best of what they used to do" is not the "best" of their deeds in contrast to the "good" among them—such that Allah, the Glorified, would distinguish between their good deeds, dividing them into good and best, then reward them for the best and cancel the good, as some have mentioned. For the context does not support this, the verses of recompense negate it, and the vast divine mercy refuses it.

Nor is what is intended by it the obligatory and recommended acts among their deeds in contrast to the permissible acts they performed, for the latter are not without some goodness, as others have mentioned.

For the speech is apparent in that what is intended is the clarification of the reward for the deeds performed in the state of patience, which are related to it. It is clear that the permissible acts performed by the one patient for Allah have no relation to his patience, so there is no reason to consider them among the deeds and then choose the best from among them.

Furthermore, a servant has no expectation that Allah will reward him for the permissible acts he performed, such that it would need to be clarified to him that the reward is in exchange for the obligatory and recommended acts he performed, which are better than the permissible acts he performed; thus, the mention of "good" would be redundant.

From this, it becomes clear that what is intended is not the supererogatory acts (النوافل) based on the lack of obligation in them, such that they would be the "best" of what he did. For the fact that the obligatory act contains more of the interest (المصلحة) necessitating goodness than the supererogatory act is known from the legislative addresses, such that there is no doubt about it.

Rather, what is intended is that the deed they perform, which has within its genus that which is good and that which is best, Allah, the Glorified, rewards it with the reward of the best individual instance of its genus. Thus, the prayer performed by the one patient for Allah, Allah, the Glorified, rewards him for it with the reward of the best individual instance of prayer, even if the one he prayed was not the best. In reality, patience requires that the deed not be scrutinized nor its specific characteristics that might imply lowliness or poor quality be accounted for, as conveyed by His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّما يُوَفَّى الصَّابِرُونَ أَجْرَهُمْ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ** — "the patient will be paid their rewards in full without any reckoning" (39:10).

It is gathered from the verse that patience for Allah necessitates the perfection of the deed. In His saying: **وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ** — "and We will surely pay them," there is a shift (التفات) from the third person to the first person plural, as has been said. What I think is that it is a return to the previous context in the verses, which was the context of the first person plural. The shift was actually in His saying a few verses prior: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسانِ** — "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and dedicated ethical conduct." The reason for this is that this verse and the verses listed up to this point contain several divine commands and prohibitions. It is more appropriate for commands and prohibitions to be attributed to the greatest and most powerful stations of their source to be reinforced by that. This is a technique used in conversations; it is said: "The King commands such-and-such," and "Your master says to you such-and-such," and it is not said: "So-and-so son of so-and-so commands or says."

Thus, it was more appropriate to attribute these obligations to the station of Majesty (الجلالة), and it was said by shifting from the first person plural to the third person: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسانِ** — "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and dedicated ethical conduct," etc. For this reason, the context continued in this manner in the following obligations as well, where it was said: **وَأَوْفُوا بِعَهْدِ اللهِ** — "Fulfill Allah’s covenant," etc., **إِنَّما يَبْلُوكُمُ اللهُ** — "Allah only tests you," etc., **وَلَوْ شاءَ اللهُ** — "Had Allah wished," etc., **وَلا تَشْتَرُوا بِعَهْدِ اللهِ** — "Do not sell Allah’s covenant," etc., **وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ باقٍ** — "and that which is with Allah is lasting."

Then it returned to the previous context, which is the first person plural, saying: **وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّ الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا** — "and We will surely pay the patient." It proceeded upon this until it reached His saying: **فَإِذا قَرَأْتَ الْقُرْآنَ** — "When you recite the Qur’an," which is a ruling, so it shifted again and said: **فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللهِ** — "seek the protection of Allah." What best illustrates the meaning we have mentioned is His saying after it: **وَإِذا بَدَّلْنا آيَةً مَكانَ آيَةٍ وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يُنَزِّلُ** — "When We change a verse in place of another verse—and Allah knows best what He sends down" (16:101), where He combined the two matters, attributing the changing of a verse in place of another to the pronoun of the first person, and the supreme knowledge to Allah, exalted is His Name.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَياةً طَيِّبَةً** — "Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall surely revive him with a good life" (16:97) to the end of the verse. This is a beautiful promise to the believers if they perform righteous deeds, and glad tidings to females that Allah does not distinguish between them and males in the acceptance of their faith, nor in the effect of their righteous deeds—which is the revival with a good life and the reward for the best of deeds—despite what most idolaters and People of the Book (Jews and Christians) built upon regarding the deprivation of women from all or most religious merits, and the lowering of her rank from that of man, placing her in a position that accepts no elevation whatsoever.

So His saying: **مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ** — "Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful" is a universal ruling of the type of establishing a rule for whoever performs righteous deeds—i.e., whoever it may be. He restricted it by his being a believer, which is in the sense of a condition (اشتراط); for the deed from one who is not a believer is nullified (حابط) and no effect follows from it, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِالْإِيمانِ فَقَدْ حَبِطَ عَمَلُهُ** — "Should anyone renounce the faith, his work shall fail" (5:5), and He said: **وَحَبِطَ ما صَنَعُوا فِيها وَباطِلٌ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "what they had wrought in it failed, and what they used to do was in vain" (11:16).

His saying: **فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَياةً طَيِّبَةً** — "We shall surely revive him with a good life": Revival (الإحياء) is the casting of life into a thing and its bestowal upon it. Thus, the sentence by its wording indicates that Allah, the Glorified, honors the believer who performs righteous deeds with a new life (حياة جديدة) other than the general life in which all other people share. What is intended is not the changing of the quality of life within him and the replacement of the foul with the good while the root of life remains as it was. Had it been so, it would have been said: "We shall surely make his life good" (فلنطيبن حياته).

Thus, the verse is similar to His saying: **أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ** — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people..." (6:122). It conveys what that verse conveys of the formation of a new, initial life.

This is not a metaphorical naming (تسمية مجازية), because the verses addressing this matter arrange upon it the effects of real life, such as His saying, the Exalted: **أُولئِكَ كَتَبَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْإِيمانَ وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِروحٍ مِنْهُ** — "He has written faith into their hearts and strengthened them with a spirit from Him" (58:22), and as His saying in the verse of *al-An'am* quoted just now: **وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ** — "and provided him with a light by which he walks among the people." For what is intended by this light is the knowledge (العلم) by which the human being is certainly guided to the truth in belief and action.

Just as he possesses knowledge and perception that others do not, he also possesses the gift of power (القدرة) to revive the truth and set aside falsehood that others do not. He, the Glorified, has said: **وَكانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — "and it was a must for Us to help the faithful" (30:47), and He said: **مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ** — "whoever has faith in Allah and the Last Day and acts righteously, they will have no fear, nor will they grieve" (5:69).

This new knowledge and power pave the way for him to see things as they truly are, so he divides them into two parts: a lasting truth (حق باق) and a fleeting falsehood (باطل فان). He turns his heart away from the fleeting falsehood, which is the worldly life with its deceptive and tempting vanities, and he takes pride in the might of Allah. Thus, Satan does not humiliate him with his whispers, nor the soul with its whims and fancies, nor the world with its splendor, because he witnesses the vanity of its goods and the transience of its blessings.

His heart becomes attached to his Lord, the Truth (الحق), who makes every truth come true by His words. He desires nothing but His countenance, loves nothing but His proximity, and fears nothing but His displeasure and distance. He sees for himself a pure, permanent, and eternal life, the affairs of which are managed only by his Lord, the All-Forgiving, the All-Loving. He faces nothing in the length of his journey except the beautiful (الحسن الجميل), for He has made beautiful everything He created, and there is nothing ugly except what Allah has made ugly of His disobedience.

This human being finds within himself such splendor, perfection, power, honor, pleasure, and joy that cannot be measured. And how could it be otherwise? He is immersed in a permanent life that has no cessation, a lasting blessing that has no exhaustion and no pain to mar it, and a goodness and happiness with which there is no wretchedness. This is what reason supports and many verses of the Qur'an speak of, which need not be cited given their abundance.

These are vital effects (آثار حيوية) that are only arranged upon a real, non-metaphorical life. Allah, the Glorified, has arranged them upon this life which He mentions and specifies for those who believe and perform righteous deeds. Thus, it is a new, real life that Allah, the Glorified, bestows upon them.

This new life specific to them is not separate from the old, shared life, even if it is other than it. The difference is in degrees (المراتب), not in number; thus, the human being does not become multiple by it. Just as the holy spirit (الروح القدسية) which Allah, the Glorified, mentions for the prophets only necessitates for them an elevation of rank, not a multiplicity of personality.

This is what reflection upon the noble verse yields, and it is a Qur'anic reality. Through this, the reason for describing it as "good" (طيبة) in His saying: **حَياةً طَيِّبَةً** — "a good life" becomes clear. It is—as has become evident—a pure life with no foulness (خبث) to corrupt it in itself or in its effect.

Exegetes have several views regarding the verse:

1. That the "good life" is the life in Paradise, where there is no death, poverty, sickness, or any other wretchedness.
2. That it is the life in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection*. Perhaps this specification comes from interpreting the end of the verse as referring to the Paradise of the Hereafter.
3. That it is the worldly life accompanied by contentment (القناعة) and satisfaction with what Allah, the Glorified, has apportioned, for that is the best of lives.
4. That it is lawful provision (الرزق الحلال), as there is no punishment for it.
5. That it is provision day by day.

The points of contention regarding these views are hardly hidden from the reflecting researcher, so we shall not prolong by citing them.

His saying: **وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْرَهُمْ بِأَحْسَنِ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "and We will surely pay them their reward by the best of what they used to do": The discussion regarding this preceded in the previous verse. In the meaning of this verse is His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ يُرْزَقُونَ فِيها بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ** — "and whoever acts righteously, whether male or female, should he be faithful—such shall enter paradise, and they will be provided therein without any reckoning" (40:40).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَإِذا قَرَأْتَ الْقُرْآنَ فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللهِ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ الرَّجِيمِ** — "When you recite the Qur’an, seek the protection of Allah from the outcast Satan" (16:98). Seeking protection (الاستعاذة) is seeking a refuge (المعاذ). The meaning is: When you recite the Qur'an, seek from Him, the Exalted, as long as you are reciting, that He protect you from the outcast Satan lest he seduce you. Thus, the commanded seeking of protection is the state of the reciter's soul as long as he recites, and he has been commanded to bring it about for himself as long as he recites. As for the reciter's saying: "I seek refuge in Allah from the outcast Satan" (أعوذ بالله من الشيطان الرجيم) or similar wording, it is a cause for bringing about the meaning of seeking protection in the soul, and is not the act itself except by a type of metaphor. He, the Glorified, said: "seek the protection of Allah" (استعذ بالله), and He did not say: "say: I seek refuge in Allah."

Through this, it appears that the view of some—that what is intended by "recitation" is the "intention" for it, making it a loose metaphor (مجاز مرسل) of the type of mentioning the effect and intending the cause—is not without some looseness.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ لَهُ سُلْطانٌ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَلى رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ** — "Indeed he has no authority over those who have faith and put their trust in their Lord" (16:99). This is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for the command mentioned in the previous verse; i.e., seek protection in Allah during recitation so that He may protect you from him, because he has no authority over one who believes in Allah and puts his trust in Him.

From the verse, it appears, firstly: that seeking protection in Allah is putting trust (التوكل) in Him, for He, the Glorified, replaced "seeking protection" in the reasoning with "putting trust" and negated his authority over those who put their trust.

Secondly: that faith (الإيمان) and trust (التوكل) are the criteria for the truth of servitude (العبودية), as in His saying, the Exalted, to Iblis: **إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ** — "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you" (13:42). He negated his authority over His servants, and He has replaced "servants" in this verse with "those who have faith and put their trust in their Lord." Reason supports this, for trust—which is handing over the reins of disposal in one's affairs to another and submitting to what he chooses for one—is the most specific effect of servitude.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **إِنَّما سُلْطانُهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُ وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِهِ مُشْرِكُونَ** — "His authority is only over those who take him for a patron and those who ascribe partners to Him" (16:100). The three singular pronouns refer to Satan. That is, the authority of Satan is restricted to those who take him as a guardian (وليا) for them who manages their affairs as he wishes, and they obey him; and to those who associate partners through him, since they take him as a guardian besides Allah and a lord obeyed other than Him. For obedience is worship (عبادة), as indicated by His saying: **أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي** — "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, 'Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. Worship Me...'" (36:60-61).

Through this, it appears, firstly: that the end of the verse explains its beginning, and that taking as a guardian one whom Allah has not permitted to be taken as such is shirk (شرك) — *associating partners with Allah* — and worship of other than Him.

Secondly: that there is no intermediary between putting trust in Allah and taking Satan as a guardian and worshipping him. Whoever does not put his trust in Allah is among the guardians of Satan.

It has been said that the singular pronoun in His saying: **وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِهِ مُشْرِكُونَ** — "and those who ascribe partners to Him" refers to Him, the Exalted. The verse would then mean that his authority is over two groups: the polytheists and those among the monotheists who take him as a guardian. However, the necessity of the difference in pronouns refutes this.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَإِذا بَدَّلْنا آيَةً مَكانَ آيَةٍ وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يُنَزِّلُ قالُوا إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُفْتَرٍ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** — "When We change a verse in place of another verse—and Allah knows best what He sends down—they say, 'You are just a fabricator!' Rather, most of them do not know" (16:101). This is an allusion to abrogation (النسخ) — *the divine replacement of a previous ruling with a new one* — and its wisdom, and an answer to what they accused him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) of regarding fabrication against Allah. It is apparent from the context of the verses that the speakers are the polytheists, even if the Jews were the most rigid in negating abrogation. It is possible that the word was something the polytheists picked up from the Jews, as they frequently consulted them regarding the matter of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

His saying: **وَإِذا بَدَّلْنا آيَةً مَكانَ آيَةٍ** — "When We change a verse in place of another verse": Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "Substitution (الإبدال), changing (التبديل), being changed (التبدل), and seeking replacement (الاستبدال) mean placing one thing in the position of another. It is more general than 'compensation' (العوض), for compensation is that the second becomes yours by giving the first. 'Changing' (التبديل) may be said for alteration (التغيير) absolutely, even if a substitute is not brought." He cited: **فَبَدَّلَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا قَوْلاً غَيْرَ الَّذِي قِيلَ لَهُمْ** — "But the wrongdoers changed the saying with other than what they had been told" (2:59), and: **فَمَنْ بَدَّلَهُ بَعْدَ ما سَمِعَهُ** — "And should anyone change it after hearing it" (2:181), and: **وَإِذا بَدَّلْنا آيَةً مَكانَ آيَةٍ** — "When We change a verse in place of another verse" (16:101), and: **بَدَّلْناهُمْ بِجَنَّتَيْهِمْ جَنَّتَيْنِ** — "and We gave them in place of their two gardens two [other] gardens" (34:16), and: **ثُمَّ بَدَّلْنا مَكانَ السَّيِّئَةِ الْحَسَنَةَ** — "Then We changed the evil [state] into a good one" (7:95).

Changing (التبديل) in the sense of alteration (التغيير) differs from changing in its well-known sense in that its first object is the one taken and sought, unlike its well-known sense. Thus, the meaning of His saying: **وَإِذا بَدَّلْنا آيَةً مَكانَ آيَةٍ** is: We placed the second verse in the position of the first through alteration, so the second, substituted verse is the one remaining and sought.

His saying: **وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يُنَزِّلُ** — "and Allah knows best what He sends down" is a metaphor for the fact that the truth has not exceeded its proper place, and that the One who sent it down is the One most worthy of sending it down, for Allah knows more about it than they do. The sentence is a circumstantial clause (حالية).

His saying: **قالُوا إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُفْتَرٍ** — "they say, 'You are just a fabricator!'": The speech is from the polytheists addressing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and accusing him of fabricating lies against Allah. For changing one saying for another, and being firm on an opinion then turning away from it, is something from which the presence of the Lord of Might is transcendent.

They exaggerated in their saying, as they did not say: "You fabricated in this change and abrogation," but rather said: **إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُفْتَرٍ** — "You are just a fabricator," restricting him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to fabrication. They used a nominal sentence and called him a fabricator. They built this on the premise that what the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) brought is of one kind, and he attributes all of it to his Lord, saying: "I am only a warner." Thus, if he were a fabricator in one thing, he would be a fabricator in everything; so he is nothing but a fabricator.

His saying: **بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** — "Rather, most of them do not know": That is, most of these polytheists who accuse you by saying, "You are just a fabricator," do not know the reality of this change and the wisdom leading to it—as will be revealed in the answer—that divine rulings follow the interests (المصالح) of the servants. Among interests are those that change with the change of situations, conditions, and times. Thus, it is necessary for the ruling to change with the change of its interest; so the ruling whose necessitating interest has departed is abrogated by another ruling whose interest has arisen.

Most of these people are heedless of this matter. As for the few among them, they are aware of the reality of the matter, even if only in general. However, they are arrogant toward the truth and antagonistic to it, and they only cast the word without regard for the side of truth.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قُلْ نَزَّلَهُ رُوحُ الْقُدُسِ مِنْ رَبِّكَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيُثَبِّتَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَهُدىً وَبُشْرى لِلْمُسْلِمِينَ** — "Say, 'The Holy Spirit has brought it down from your Lord with the truth, to strengthen those who have faith, and as a guidance and good news for the Muslims'" (16:102). An allusion to the meaning of "Spirit" (الروح) preceded at the beginning of the Surah. "Holiness" (القدس) is purity and transcendence. It is apparent that the genitive construction is for specification, meaning a spirit pure from the defilements of matter, transcendent above error, mistake, and misguidance. He is the one called in another place in His speech "the Trustworthy Spirit" (الروح الأمين), and in another place "Gabriel" (جبريل) among the angels. He, the Exalted, said: **نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ** — "The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down to your heart" (26:193-194), and He said: **مَنْ كانَ عَدُوًّا لِجِبْرِيلَ فَإِنَّهُ نَزَّلَهُ عَلى قَلْبِكَ** — "Whoever is an enemy to Gabriel [should know that] it is he who has brought it down to your heart" (2:97).

So His saying: **قُلْ نَزَّلَهُ رُوحُ الْقُدُسِ مِنْ رَبِّكَ** — "Say, 'The Holy Spirit has brought it down from your Lord'" is a command for the answer. What first comes to mind is that the pronoun refers to the Qur'an from the perspective of its being an abrogator—i.e., the abrogating verse. It is possible that it refers to the Qur'an absolutely. In the use of the intensive form "brought down" (تنزيل) rather than "sent down" (إنزال), there is an allusion to gradualness (التدريج).

The natural flow of the speech would have been to say: "from my Lord" (من ربي), but it was turned away from that to His saying: **مِنْ رَبِّكَ** — "from your Lord" to indicate the perfection of care and mercy toward him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). It is as if He does not accept the interruption of His address to him, so He seizes the opportunity to speak to him wherever possible, and to indicate that what is intended by the commanded "saying" is informing them of that, not merely pronouncing these words. So understand.

His saying: **لِيُثَبِّتَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** — "to strengthen those who have faith": Strengthening (التثبيت) is making steadfastness firm and emphasizing it by casting steadfastness after steadfastness upon them. It is as if by the root of their faith in Allah, His Messenger, and the Last Day, they became steady upon the truth. By the renewal of the ruling according to the renewal of the interest, they are given steadfastness upon steadfastness, without their first steadfastness weakening by continuing upon actions that do not match the interest of the time. For it is clear that one who is commanded to follow a path for the interest of a goal, and takes to following it out of faith in the guiding command, covering a portion of it according to what he is commanded out of regard for the interest of the goal—whether quickly or slowly, by night or by day—then the nature of the interest changes: if the guiding command did not change the manner of following and he continued upon his previous command, the faith of the follower would weaken and its pillars would be stripped away. But if he is commanded with a new manner of following that agrees with the interest and guarantees happiness, his faith increases in steadfastness upon steadfastness.

Thus, in the bringing down of the Qur'an with abrogation and the renewal of the ruling according to the renewal of the interest, there is a strengthening for those who believe and a giving to them of steadfastness upon steadfastness.

His saying: **وَهُدىً وَبُشْرى لِلْمُسْلِمِينَ** — "and as a guidance and good news for the Muslims": These are those who submit (يسلمون) the ruling to Allah without objection. Thus, the abrogating verse for them is a showing of the way and glad tidings of happiness and Paradise.

The differentiation of effects—by specifying steadfastness (التثبيت) for the believers (المؤمنين), and guidance (الهدى) and good news (البشرى) for the Muslims (المسلمين)—is only due to the distinction between faith (الإيمان) and submission (الإسلام). Faith pertains to the heart, and its portion is steadfastness in knowledge and conviction; whereas submission pertains to outward action and the stage of the physical limbs, and its portion is being guided toward obligatory deeds, and the good news that the ultimate end is Paradise and felicity.

Some discussion regarding abrogation (النسخ) has already passed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **مَا نَنْسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنْسِهَا نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِنْهَا أَوْ مِثْلِهَا** "Any verse that We abrogate or cause to be forgotten, We bring another which is better than it, or one like it" (2:106), in the first volume of this book.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ** "We certainly know that they say, ‘It is only a human that teaches him.’" This is another fabrication of theirs against the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, namely their saying: **إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ** "It is only a human that teaches him." As suggested by the context of their objection and the response given to it, there was a non-Arab (أعجمي) man there, whose speech was not eloquent, who possessed some knowledge of religious doctrines and prophetic traditions. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله would perhaps meet him, so they accused him of taking what he claimed to be revelation from this man, and that it was this man who was teaching him. This is what Allah, the Exalted, recounted of their saying: **إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ** "It is only a human that teaches him." There is a conciseness (إيجاز) in this statement, the implied meaning being: "It is only a human who teaches him, and he attributes what he learns from him to Allah as a fabrication against Him." This is evident.

It is well known that responding to this by merely stating that the man’s tongue is non-Arab while the Quran is clear Arabic does not uproot the doubt from its source. This is because it is possible for the man to convey the subject matter to him in his non-Arab tongue, and then he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—through the eloquence of his speech—casts it into the mold of eloquent Arabic. In fact, this is the first thing that comes to mind from their saying: **إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ** "It is only a human that teaches him," since they expressed this using the term "teaching" (التعليم) rather than "dictation" (التلقين) or "recitation" (الإملاء), and teaching is closer to meanings (المعاني) than it is to words (الألفاظ).

From this, it becomes clear that His saying: **لِسَانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ** "The tongue of him they hint at..." until His saying "...clear (مُبِينٌ)" is not the sole response to their doubt; rather, the speech that follows it until the end of the two subsequent verses completes the response.

The summary of the response, derived from all three verses, is: As for your accusation that a human teaches him and then he attributes it to Allah as a fabrication—if you mean that he teaches him the Quran in its literal wording by dictating it to him, and that the Quran is his speech and not the speech of Allah, then the answer is that this man’s tongue is non-Arab (أعجمي) while this Quran is clear Arabic (عربي مبين).

And if you mean that the man teaches him the meanings of the Quran—while the wording is inevitably the Prophet’s صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—and he attributes it to Allah as a fabrication against Him, then the answer is that what the Quran contains are true insights (معارف حقة) in which no person of intellect (ذو لب) can doubt, and which the intellects are compelled to accept. Allah has guided the Prophet to them, so he is a believer in the signs of Allah; for had he not been a believer, Allah would not have guided him, and Allah does not guide those who do not believe in His signs. Since he is a believer in the signs of Allah, he does not fabricate lies against Allah, for none fabricate lies against Him except those who do not believe in His signs. Thus, this Quran is not a fabrication, nor is it taken from a human and falsely attributed to Allah, the Glorified.

So His saying: **لِسَانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجَمِيٌّ وَهَذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُبِينٌ** "The tongue of him they hint at is non-Arab, and this is a clear Arabic tongue" is a response to the first part of the doubt, which is that the Quran in its wording was taken from a human through dictation. The meaning is: the tongue of the man toward whom they hint (يُلْحِدُونَ)—meaning they incline toward him and intend him by their saying: **إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ** "It is only a human that teaches him"—is non-Arab (أعجمي), meaning it is not eloquent or clear; whereas this Quran recited to you is a clear Arabic tongue. How can the production of an eloquent Arabic discourse be imagined from a man whose tongue is non-Arab?

And His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ** "Indeed those who do not believe..." until the end of the two verses is a response to the second part of the doubt, which is that he learns the meanings from him and then attributes them to Allah as a fabrication.

The meaning is: Indeed, those who do not believe in the signs of Allah and disbelieve in them, Allah does not guide them to Himself or to His manifest true insights, and for them is a painful punishment. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is a believer in the signs of Allah because he is guided by the guidance of Allah. Only those who do not believe in the signs of Allah fabricate lies and attribute them to Allah, and it is they who are the liars, persisting in falsehood. As for the likes of the Prophet, who believes in the signs of Allah, he does not fabricate lies nor does he lie. Thus, the two verses are an allusion (كناية) to the fact that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is guided by the guidance of Allah and is a believer in His signs, and one like him does not fabricate or lie.

The commentators (المفسرون) have severed these two verses from the first verse, making the first verse the complete response to the doubt. However, you have seen that it does not suffice as a full response.

Furthermore, they interpreted His saying: **وَهَذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُبِينٌ** "and this is a clear Arabic tongue" as a challenge (تحدي) regarding the miraculous nature (إعجاز) of the Quran in its eloquence. Yet you know that there is no mention in the wording of the verse that the Quran is miraculous in its eloquence, nor is there any trace of a challenge. The extent of what is in it is that it is clear Arabic, and there is no way for a non-Arab to express it or articulate it.

Then they interpreted the two following verses as a threat to those disbelievers in the signs of Allah who accused His Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله of fabrication, promising them a painful punishment, and turning the accusation of fabrication and lying back upon them—that they are more deserving of fabrication and lying since they do not believe in the signs of Allah, for Allah has not guided them.

They then spoke, based on this premise, regarding the individual words of the two verses in a manner that further distances them from the true meaning. You have already seen that this leads to the response being insufficient in uprooting the objection from its source.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad narrated from ‘Uthman ibn Abi al-‘As, who said: "I was sitting with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله when his gaze became fixed. He then said: 'Gabriel came to me and commanded me to place this verse in this position of the Surah: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسَانِ** "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness..." until His saying "...take admonition (تَذَكَّرُونَ)."'"

I say: It was also narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas from ‘Uthman ibn Maz‘un رضي الله عنه.

In *al-Majma‘*: A narration states that ‘Uthman ibn Maz‘un said: "I had embraced Islam out of shyness before the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—due to how often he would present Islam to me—but Islam had not settled in my heart. One day, I was with him while he was in a state of contemplation. His gaze turned toward the sky as if he were seeking to understand something. When that state left him, I asked him about his condition. He said: 'Yes, while I was speaking to you, I saw Gabriel in the air. He brought me this verse: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسَانِ** "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness..."' He recited it to me until its end, and then Islam settled in my heart.

I went to his uncle Abu Talib and informed him. He said: 'O people of Quraysh, follow Muhammad and you will be guided, for he only commands you toward noble character (مكارم الأخلاق).' I then went to al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah and recited this verse to him. He said: 'If Muhammad said it, then how excellent is what he said; and if his Lord said it, then how excellent is what He said.' He said: So Allah revealed: **أَفَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي تَوَلَّى وَأَعْطَى قَلِيلاً وَأَكْدَى** 'Did you see him who turned away, and gave a little, then stopped?'" (the tradition continues).

In the same source, from ‘Ikrimah, who said: "The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recited this verse to al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah. He said: 'O son of my brother, repeat it.' So he repeated it. He said: 'Indeed, it has a sweetness, and it has a grace; its top is fruitful and its bottom is lush, and it is not the speech of a human.'"

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, with its chain of narration from Isma‘il ibn Muslim from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام regarding the verse: "Allah has no command for His servants except justice (العدل) and kindness (الإحسان)."

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Ibn Babawayh with his chain of narration from ‘Amr ibn ‘Uthman, who said: "‘Ali عليه‌السلام came out to his companions while they were discussing chivalry (المروءة). He said: 'Where do you stand regarding the Book of Allah?' They said: 'O Commander of the Faithful, in which place?' He said: 'In His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسَانِ** "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness." Justice (العدل) is fairness (الإنصاف), and kindness (الإحسان) is graciousness (التفضل).'"

I say: It was narrated by al-‘Ayyashi from ‘Amr ibn ‘Uthman al-‘Asi from him عليه‌السلام. It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn al-Najjar in his history via the route of al-‘Ukli from his father from him عليه‌السلام, and its wording is: "‘Ali ibn Abi Talib passed by a group of people talking. He said: 'What are you engaged in?' They said: 'We are discussing chivalry.' He said: 'Has Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, not sufficed you of that in His Book when He says: **إِنَّ اللهَ يَأْمُرُ بِالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسَانِ** "Indeed Allah enjoins justice and kindness"? Justice is fairness and kindness is graciousness.'"

I say: In several narrations, justice (العدل) has been interpreted as monotheism (التوحيد) or the two testimonies of faith (الشهادتين), and kindness (الإحسان) as the mastership (الولاية). In others, the prohibition of breaking covenants is linked to the obligation of remaining steadfast upon the mastership.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **مَنْ عَمِلَ صَالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثَى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَيَاةً طَيِّبَةً** "Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall surely revive him with a good life" (16:97), he said: "He عليه‌السلام said: 'Contentment (القنوع).'"

In *al-Ma‘ani*, with its chain of narration from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr from some of his companions from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "It was said to him: 'Abu al-Khattab mentions from you that you said: "Once you know the truth, do as you wish."' He said: 'May Allah curse Abu al-Khattab! By Allah, I did not say it like that. Rather, I said to him: "Once you know the truth, do whatever good you wish, and it will be accepted from you." For Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: **مَنْ عَمِلَ صَالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثَى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولَئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ يُرْزَقُونَ فِيهَا بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ** "Whoever acts righteously, whether male or female, should he be faithful—such shall enter paradise, and they will be provided therein without any reckoning" (40:40), and He says: **مَنْ عَمِلَ صَالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثَى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَيَاةً طَيِّبَةً** "Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall surely revive him with a good life" (16:97).'"

I say: This is what we previously presented regarding the meaning of the verse.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Abu Basir from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "I said to him: **فَإِذَا قَرَأْتَ الْقُرْآنَ فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللهِ** "When you recite the Quran, seek the protection of Allah..." until His saying "...trust (يَتَوَكَّلُونَ)." He said: 'O Muhammad, by Allah, he [Satan] may be given authority over the body of a believer, but he is not given authority over his religion. He was given authority over Job (Ayub) and his physical form was disfigured, but he was not given authority over his religion. He may be given authority over the bodies of believers, but he is not given authority over their religion.'

I said to him: 'His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **إِنَّمَا سُلْطَانُهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يَتَوَلَّوْنَهُ وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِهِ مُشْرِكُونَ** "His authority is only over those who befriend him and those who make him a partner [of Allah]" (16:100)?' He said: 'Those who associate partners with Allah—he is given authority over their bodies and over their religions.'"

I say: It was narrated by al-‘Ayyashi from Abu Basir from him (peace be upon him). Returning the pronoun in "with Him" (بِهِ) to Allah is one of the two meanings in the verse.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Hakim narrated and authenticated it, as did al-Bayhaqi in *Shu‘ab al-Iman*, from Ibn ‘Abbas: "Regarding His saying: **إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ** 'It is only a human that teaches him,' he said: 'They said: "‘Abdah ibn al-Hadrami, who is a man of books, is only teaching Muhammad." So Allah said: **لِسَانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجَمِيٌّ وَهَذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُبِينٌ** "The tongue of him they hint at is non-Arab, and this is a clear Arabic tongue."'"

In *Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi*, from Muhammad ibn ‘Azamah al-Sayrafi from someone who informed him from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, created the Holy Spirit (روح القدس), and He did not create a creation closer to Allah than it, though it is not the most noble of His creation to Him. When He intends a matter, He casts it to it, and it casts it to the stars, and so it proceeds. His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّمَا يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ لِسَانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ** 'We certainly know that they say, "It is only a human that teaches him." The tongue of him they hint at...'—it was the tongue of Abu Fakihah, the client (مولى) of the Banu al-Hadrami. He was non-Arab (أعجمي) of tongue, and he had followed the Prophet of Allah and believed in him, and he was from the People of the Book. So Quraysh said: 'This man, by Allah, teaches Muhammad his knowledge in his tongue.' Allah says: **وَهَذَا لِسَانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُبِينٌ** 'and this is a clear Arabic tongue.'"

I say: The narrations regarding this man differ. In this narration, he is Abu Fakihah, the client of the Banu al-Hadrami. In the previous narration, he is ‘Abdah ibn al-Hadrami. From Qatadah, it is said he was ‘Abdah ibn al-Hadrami and was called Maqis. From al-Suddi, it is said he was a Christian slave of the Banu al-Hadrami who had read the Torah and the Gospel, called Abu Bishr. From Mujahid, it is said he was the son of al-Hadrami, a non-Arab who spoke Roman (Byzantine Greek). From Ibn ‘Abbas, in another narration, it is said he was a blacksmith in Mecca named Bal‘am, who was non-Arab of tongue; the polytheists would see the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله entering and leaving his presence, so they said: "Only Bal‘am teaches him."

What is certain from their contents is that he was a Roman man, a client of the Banu al-Hadrami living in Mecca, a Christian with expertise in the books of the People of the Book, whom they accused of teaching the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from al-Dahhak regarding the verse: "They used to say: 'Only Salman the Persian teaches him.' So Allah revealed: **لِسَانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجَمِيٌّ** 'The tongue of him they hint at is non-Arab.'"

I say: This does not align with the verse being Meccan.

In the same source, Ibn al-Khara’iti in *Masawi al-Akhlaq* and Ibn ‘Asakir in his history narrated from ‘Abdullah ibn Jarad: "He asked the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: 'Does a believer commit adultery?' He said: 'That may happen.' He asked: 'Does a believer steal?' He said: 'That may happen.' He asked: 'Does a believer lie?' He said: 'No.' Then the Prophet of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله followed it with: **إِنَّمَا يَفْتَرِي الْكَذِبَ الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ** 'Only they fabricate lies who do not believe...'"

In *Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi*, from al-‘Abbas ibn al-Hilal from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida عليه‌السلام: "He mentioned a man who was a liar, then said: 'Allah said: **إِنَّمَا يَفْتَرِي الْكَذِبَ الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ** "Only they fabricate lies who do not believe..."'"
